The 2026 national median cost is about $6,690 per month (A Place for Mom). Medicare does not cover room and board. To find and compare options near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.heroSubhead: Compare nearby memory care communities, understand real 2026 costs, and learn every way to pay — with free, no-pressure help from a licensed team.
pillar.intro: Choosing memory care for a parent or spouse with Alzheimer's or dementia is one of the hardest decisions a family faces. This guide explains what memory care is, what it costs in 2026, and how 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you find the right community near you.

sections.h2: What is a memory care facility?
sections.body: A memory care facility is a residential community designed specifically for people living with Alzheimer's disease, dementia, or other cognitive impairment. Unlike standard assisted living, memory care adds secured (often locked or alarmed) entrances to prevent wandering, staff trained in dementia behaviors, structured daily routines, and higher staff-to-resident ratios. Most communities provide 24-hour supervision, help with bathing, dressing, and medication, plus therapies designed to slow decline and reduce agitation. Memory care can be a standalone building or a dedicated, secured wing inside an assisted living or continuing care community. The goal is safety, dignity, and quality of life as cognitive needs increase.

sections.h2: How much does memory care cost in 2026?
sections.body: Memory care typically runs $4,000 to $11,000 per month in 2026, with a national median near $6,690 (A Place for Mom). Other 2026 estimates range from a $6,450 median up to roughly $7,645–$8,019, reflecting different survey methods. Memory care generally costs 15–25% more than standard assisted living because of secured environments, specialized staffing, and enhanced therapies. Your actual price depends on location, the community's care level, room type (private vs. shared), and how much hands-on help your loved one needs. Always ask whether the quoted rate is all-inclusive or whether care levels add fees on top of base rent.

sections.h2: Does Medicare or Medicaid pay for memory care?
sections.body: Medicare does not pay for the room, board, or custodial (daily living) costs of a memory care facility, because it classifies these as long-term custodial care rather than medical care. Medicare does still cover medically necessary services your loved one receives — doctor visits, cognitive assessments, prescriptions, short-term skilled nursing after a qualifying hospital stay (up to 100 days), and part-time home health. Medicaid is different: through Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers, many states help pay for memory care for residents who meet income and care-need limits. Eligibility and covered settings vary by state, so verify your state's rules.

sections.h2: How do families pay for memory care?
sections.body: Most families combine several sources. Common ones include personal savings and retirement income, the sale or rental of a home, long-term care insurance, and life insurance conversions. Wartime veterans and surviving spouses may qualify for the VA Aid and Attendance pension, which adds monthly funds (via VA Form 21-2680) that can be applied to memory care. Medicaid HCBS waivers help lower-income seniors in many states. A reverse mortgage or bridge loan can cover gaps while a home sells. Because the right mix depends on your finances and timeline, planning early protects more of your family's money.

sections.h2: How 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you find memory care near you
sections.body: 1-800-MEDIGAP is the trusted toll-free line for all things senior in America. One call connects you with a knowledgeable team that helps you locate memory care communities near you, understand what each charges and includes, and sort through how Medicare, Medicaid, VA benefits, and insurance fit your situation. We do not sell you a building — we help you make a confident, informed choice. If your loved one's Medicare or Medigap coverage needs review as care needs change, we handle that too. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to get started today.

faqs.q: What is the difference between memory care and assisted living?
faqs.a: Assisted living helps generally independent seniors with daily tasks. Memory care adds secured, wander-safe environments, dementia-trained staff, structured routines, and higher supervision for people with Alzheimer's or dementia. Because of this specialized care, memory care typically costs 15–25% more than assisted living. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare options.

faqs.q: How much does memory care cost per month in 2026?
faqs.a: In 2026, memory care typically costs $4,000 to $11,000 per month, with a national median near $6,690 (A Place for Mom). Price depends on location, room type, and care level. For help comparing real costs near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover memory care facilities?
faqs.a: No. Medicare does not pay for the room, board, or daily custodial care in a memory care facility. It does cover medically necessary services such as doctor visits, cognitive assessments, prescriptions, and up to 100 days of skilled nursing after a qualifying hospital stay. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review coverage.

faqs.q: How do I find memory care facilities near me?
faqs.a: Start by listing your loved one's care needs, budget, and preferred location, then tour communities and ask what each rate includes. The fastest way is to call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) — our team helps you locate, compare, and vet memory care options near you for free.

faqs.q: Can Medicaid pay for memory care?
faqs.a: Often yes. Through Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers, many states help cover memory care for seniors who meet income and care-need requirements. Covered settings and waiting lists vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn what your state offers and how to apply.

faqs.q: When is it time to move someone to memory care?
faqs.a: Consider memory care when safety risks rise — wandering, leaving the stove on, frequent falls, missed medications, aggression, or caregiver burnout. If your loved one can no longer be safely supervised at home, it may be time. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to talk through the signs and your options.

faqs.q: Do veterans get help paying for memory care?
faqs.a: Yes. Wartime veterans and surviving spouses who need help with daily activities may qualify for the VA Aid and Attendance pension, which adds monthly funds (applied via VA Form 21-2680) that can be used for memory care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see if your family qualifies.

faqs.q: Is memory care the same as a nursing home?
faqs.a: No. Memory care is residential care focused on dementia and Alzheimer's, with security and trained staff but not constant medical care. Nursing homes provide round-the-clock skilled medical care for complex health needs. Some seniors move from memory care to a nursing home later. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare.

sections.h2: How do I find memory care facilities near me?
sections.body: Start with three things: your loved one's specific care needs (wandering, medication, mobility), your monthly budget, and the location families want for easy visits. Then build a short list, tour each community, and ask exactly what the base rate covers versus what triggers added care-level fees. Look for secured entrances, dementia-trained staff, clear staffing ratios, and a calm, clean environment. Reading state inspection reports helps too. The simplest path is one phone call: 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) connects you with a team that helps locate and compare memory care facilities near you at no cost.

sections.h2: What should I look for when touring?
sections.body: On a tour, check that exits are secured to prevent wandering, that staff are specifically trained in dementia care, and ask the actual staff-to-resident ratio during day and night shifts. Observe whether current residents look clean, calm, and engaged. Ask how the community handles agitation, falls, medical emergencies, and end-of-life care. Request the full fee schedule in writing, including how care levels raise the monthly cost over time. Trust your instincts about cleanliness, smell, and how staff speak to residents — these everyday signals reveal more than the brochure. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP if you want a checklist to bring along.

faqs.q: How do I find the best memory care facility near me?
faqs.a: Define care needs and budget, tour secured communities, verify dementia-trained staffing, and get all fees in writing. Reviews and state inspection reports help confirm quality. For free help locating and comparing memory care near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: How much do memory care facilities cost near me?
faqs.a: Costs vary by region but run $4,000 to $11,000 per month in 2026, with a national median near $6,690 (A Place for Mom). Urban and coastal areas trend higher. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to get cost estimates for facilities near you.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for memory care facilities?

faqs.q: How quickly can someone move into memory care?
faqs.a: Some communities admit within days if a bed is open and assessments are complete; others have waitlists. Having medical records and a care assessment ready speeds things up. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find communities with current availability near you.

sections.h2: What does memory care near me include?
sections.body: Memory care near you typically includes a secured residence to prevent wandering, 24-hour supervision, dementia-trained staff, help with bathing, dressing, grooming, and medications, three meals plus snacks, housekeeping and laundry, and structured activities designed to keep residents engaged and calm. Many communities add cognitive therapies and family support programs. What varies most is the care-level structure: some quote one all-inclusive rate, while others charge a base rent plus tiered fees as needs grow. Always confirm what is included before comparing prices. For help sorting this out near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How do I choose between nearby communities?
sections.body: Compare communities on four points: safety (secured exits, staffing ratios), specialization (true dementia training, not just assisted living), cost transparency (all fees in writing), and location (close enough for regular visits, which improves resident wellbeing). Tour at different times of day, talk to staff and families, and review state inspection records. A community a few minutes farther away may be worth it if the care is stronger. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you weigh these trade-offs and shortlist the best memory care near you, free of charge.

page.intro: Memory care cost is the first question most families ask. This page breaks down 2026 prices, what drives them, and how 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you pay.

sections.h2: What does memory care cost in 2026?
sections.body: In 2026, memory care typically costs $4,000 to $11,000 per month. National estimates vary by survey: A Place for Mom reports a median near $6,690, while U.S. News estimates an average around $7,645, and SeniorLiving.org cites figures up to roughly $8,019. The wide range reflects location, room type, and care intensity. Memory care generally costs 15–25% more than standard assisted living because of secured environments, dementia-trained staff, and enhanced therapies. When comparing quotes, confirm whether the price is all-inclusive or whether tiered care levels add monthly fees. For help estimating your real cost, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What drives memory care costs up or down?
sections.body: Several factors move the price: geographic location (coastal and metro areas cost more), private versus shared rooms, the community's care-level structure, and how much hands-on help your loved one needs. Newer communities with more amenities and lower staff-to-resident ratios charge more. Costs also tend to rise over time as cognitive needs increase and care levels step up. Asking for a complete fee schedule — base rent plus every add-on — prevents surprises. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you compare apples to apples across communities and find options that fit your budget.

faqs.q: How much does memory care cost per month?
faqs.a: Memory care costs $4,000 to $11,000 per month in 2026, with a national median near $6,690 (A Place for Mom). Your price depends on location, room type, and care level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for estimates near you.

faqs.q: Why does memory care cost more than assisted living?
faqs.a: Memory care costs 15–25% more because it adds secured, wander-safe environments, dementia-trained staff, higher supervision ratios, and specialized therapies. These extras protect safety and quality of life. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare both options and their costs.

faqs.q: Does Medicare reduce memory care cost?
faqs.a: Medicare doesn't pay for memory care room and board, so it won't lower that cost. It does cover related medical services like doctor visits and short-term skilled nursing after a hospital stay. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to review coverage.

faqs.q: How can I lower memory care costs?
faqs.a: Options include shared rooms, communities in lower-cost areas, Medicaid HCBS waivers, VA Aid and Attendance for veterans, and long-term care insurance. Comparing all-inclusive versus tiered pricing also helps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help finding affordable options.

faqs.q: Do memory care costs increase over time?
faqs.a: Often yes. Many communities use care levels, so monthly costs rise as cognitive and physical needs grow. Annual rate increases are also common. Ask for the full fee structure upfront. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare transparent pricing near you.

page.heroSubhead: Understand exactly what Medicare does and doesn't pay for memory care — and what fills the gap.
page.intro: Families often assume Medicare covers memory care. This page explains what's actually covered, what isn't, and how 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you pay for the rest.

sections.h2: What does Medicare not cover for memory care?
sections.body: Medicare does not pay for the core costs of a memory care facility — room and board, personal care like bathing and dressing, medication reminders, and 24-hour supervision — because it classifies these as custodial long-term care rather than medical treatment. This is true even when the resident has Alzheimer's or dementia. That means the bulk of a memory care bill, often $6,000 or more per month in 2026, is not a Medicare benefit. Families typically cover it through savings, long-term care insurance, Medicaid, or VA benefits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to map out how to pay.

sections.h2: What medical care does Medicare still cover?
sections.body: Even in a memory care setting, Medicare continues to cover medically necessary care. This includes doctor visits, cognitive assessments and care planning, prescription drugs (through Part D), medically necessary part-time home health, hospice, and up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care following a qualifying inpatient hospital stay. A Medigap (Medicare Supplement) plan can reduce the out-of-pocket share of these covered medical services. So while Medicare won't pay the facility's monthly rent, it remains important for your loved one's ongoing health costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to make sure their Medicare and Medigap coverage is working as hard as possible.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for memory care?
faqs.a: No. Medicare does not cover memory care room, board, or custodial daily care. It does cover medically necessary services like doctor visits, cognitive assessments, prescriptions, and up to 100 days of skilled nursing after a qualifying hospital stay. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover dementia or Alzheimer's care?
faqs.a: Medicare covers the medical side of dementia care — diagnosis, cognitive assessments, care planning, and medications — but not the custodial room-and-board cost of a memory care facility. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn what fills the gap.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover skilled nursing for dementia patients?
faqs.a: Yes, up to 100 days. After a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least three days, Medicare covers medically necessary skilled nursing facility care for up to 100 days, with cost-sharing after day 20. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for details.

faqs.q: If Medicare won't pay, who does?
faqs.a: Families typically use personal savings, long-term care insurance, Medicaid HCBS waivers, and VA Aid and Attendance for veterans. Each has rules and timelines. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help building a payment plan for memory care.

faqs.q: Does a Medigap plan help with memory care?
faqs.a: A Medigap plan lowers out-of-pocket costs on Medicare-covered medical services, including doctor visits and skilled nursing, but it does not pay memory care room and board. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to review your Medigap coverage.

sections.h2: How much does memory care cost in 2026?
sections.body: Memory care costs $4,000 to $11,000 per month in 2026. The national median is roughly $6,690 per month according to A Place for Mom, which works out to about $267 per day. U.S. News estimates a national average closer to $7,645, and SeniorLiving.org reports figures as high as $8,019, depending on methodology. Costs land where they do based on geography, private versus shared rooms, and the level of hands-on care required. Because memory care provides secured environments and specialized staffing, it runs 15–25% higher than standard assisted living. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for estimates tailored to your area.

sections.h2: What's included in the monthly cost?
sections.body: A memory care monthly rate usually covers a secured room, meals, housekeeping, laundry, 24-hour supervision, help with daily activities, medication management, and structured cognitive activities. What's often not included — and can raise the bill — are higher care levels as needs increase, incontinence supplies, salon services, and certain therapies. Some communities bundle everything into one rate; others charge a base rent plus à-la-carte fees. Always request a complete written fee schedule so you can compare communities accurately. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you read the fine print and avoid surprise costs.

faqs.a: At the 2026 national median of about $6,690 per month, memory care runs roughly $267 per day. Daily costs vary widely by location and care level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare local pricing.

faqs.q: Why is memory care so expensive?
faqs.a: Memory care costs more because it provides secured, wander-safe buildings, dementia-trained staff, higher supervision ratios, and specialized therapies — all of which are labor-intensive. This is why it runs 15–25% above assisted living. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for affordable options.

faqs.q: Does Medicare lower how much memory care costs?

sections.h2: What are the main ways to pay for memory care?
sections.body: Most families combine several sources. Private funds come from savings, retirement income, pensions, and selling or renting a home. Long-term care insurance, if purchased earlier, can pay a large share. Life insurance policies can sometimes be converted to cash for care. For lower-income seniors, Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers help in many states. Wartime veterans and surviving spouses may qualify for the VA Aid and Attendance pension. Bridge loans or reverse mortgages can cover gaps while a home sells. The right mix depends on your finances — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to build a plan.

sections.h2: How do veterans and Medicaid help pay?
sections.body: Veterans: The VA Aid and Attendance pension adds monthly funds for wartime veterans and surviving spouses who need help with daily activities. You apply using VA Form 21-2680, and the benefit can be applied to memory care. Medicaid: Through HCBS waivers, many states pay for memory care services for residents who meet income, asset, and care-need limits — though covered settings and waitlists vary by state. Both programs have detailed eligibility rules, and applying correctly the first time saves months. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you understand whether your family qualifies and how to start.

faqs.q: How do most families pay for memory care?
faqs.a: Most combine personal savings, home sale proceeds, long-term care insurance, Medicaid HCBS waivers, and VA Aid and Attendance for veterans. Medicare doesn't cover room and board. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for a free payment plan.

faqs.q: Does the VA help pay for memory care?
faqs.a: Yes. Wartime veterans and surviving spouses who need help with daily activities may qualify for the VA Aid and Attendance pension (via VA Form 21-2680), which can be applied to memory care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check eligibility.

faqs.q: Can Medicaid pay for memory care?
faqs.a: Often yes. Many states use Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers to cover memory care for income-eligible seniors, though settings and waitlists vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to learn your state's rules.

faqs.q: Does long-term care insurance cover memory care?
faqs.a: Usually yes, if the policy was purchased before diagnosis. Most long-term care policies cover memory care once the person needs help with daily activities or has cognitive impairment. Check your policy's benefit limits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help reviewing coverage.

faqs.q: Can I use a reverse mortgage to pay for memory care?
faqs.a: Possibly, if a spouse still lives in the home. A reverse mortgage or bridge loan can fund care while assets are arranged or a home sells. These carry costs and risks, so weigh them carefully. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to discuss options.

page.h1: When to Move to Memory Care
page.quickAnswer: Consider memory care when safety risks rise — wandering, leaving the stove on, frequent falls, missed medications, aggression, or caregiver burnout. If a loved one can no longer be safely supervised at home, it's likely time. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to talk through the signs and your options.
page.heroSubhead: Recognize the warning signs that it's time for memory care — and act with confidence.
page.intro: Knowing when to move a loved one to memory care is rarely obvious. This page covers the key signs and how 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you decide and act.

sections.h2: What are the signs it's time for memory care?
sections.body: Watch for safety and care signals: wandering or getting lost, leaving the stove on or doors unlocked, frequent falls, missed or doubled medications, rapid weight loss from forgetting to eat, worsening confusion, aggression or sundowning, and incontinence that's hard to manage at home. Equally important is caregiver burnout — when a spouse or adult child can no longer provide safe, around-the-clock supervision without harming their own health. No single sign means it's time, but a cluster of them usually does. If you're seeing several, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to talk through the decision.

sections.h2: How do I make the move easier?
sections.body: Once you've decided, plan ahead. Tour and choose a community before a crisis forces a rushed move. Involve your loved one as much as their condition allows, and keep the conversation calm and reassuring. Bring familiar items — photos, a favorite chair, bedding — to make the new room feel like home. Coordinate medical records and medications in advance, and lean on the community's transition team. Expect an adjustment period of a few weeks. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you find the right community and time the move so it's safe, dignified, and as smooth as possible.

faqs.q: When should someone move to memory care?
faqs.a: Move to memory care when safety risks like wandering, falls, missed medications, or aggression appear, or when a caregiver can no longer provide safe 24-hour supervision. A cluster of these signs usually means it's time. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to discuss.

faqs.q: What are the warning signs of dementia progression?
faqs.a: Warning signs include getting lost in familiar places, leaving appliances on, frequent falls, weight loss, worsening confusion, agitation, and difficulty with hygiene. These often signal that home care is no longer safe. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance on next steps.

faqs.q: Is it too early to consider memory care?
faqs.a: It's rarely too early to plan. Touring communities and understanding costs before a crisis gives you better choices and less stress. Many families wish they had started sooner. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to begin planning.

faqs.q: How do I know if I'm experiencing caregiver burnout?
faqs.a: Signs include exhaustion, resentment, neglecting your own health, anxiety, and feeling unable to keep your loved one safe. Burnout puts both of you at risk and is a valid reason to consider memory care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for support and options.

page.heroSubhead: See the latest 2026 average memory care costs nationally and by region — and how to cover them.
page.intro: The average cost of memory care helps families budget and plan. This page shares current 2026 figures and how 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you pay.

sections.h2: What is the average cost of memory care in 2026?
sections.body: The national average cost of memory care in 2026 falls between roughly $6,450 and $8,019 per month, depending on the source. A Place for Mom reports a median near $6,690, U.S. News estimates an average around $7,645, and SeniorLiving.org cites figures up to $8,019. State-level prices range from under $5,000 in lower-cost regions to more than $11,000 in high-cost metros. These averages reflect base care; private rooms and higher care levels push costs above the median. For an estimate that reflects your specific location and needs, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Why do average costs vary so much by state?
sections.body: Memory care averages swing widely because labor, real estate, and regulation differ by state. Coastal and northeastern states tend to run highest, while parts of the South and Midwest sit below the national median. Within a state, urban areas cost more than rural ones. Care level matters too: a resident needing extensive hands-on help pays more than one in early-stage decline. That's why a single national average only gets you so far. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you find the real range for your area and identify communities that fit your budget.

faqs.q: What is the average cost of memory care per month?
faqs.a: In 2026, the average cost of memory care is about $6,690 per month nationally (A Place for Mom median), with other estimates up to $7,645 (U.S. News). State ranges span under $5,000 to over $11,000. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: Which states have the highest memory care costs?
faqs.a: Coastal and northeastern states generally have the highest memory care costs, often exceeding $9,000–$11,000 per month, while parts of the South and Midwest fall below the national median. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for averages in your state.

faqs.q: Is the average cost of memory care rising?
faqs.a: Yes. Memory care costs have risen steadily due to labor shortages, inflation, and growing demand. Many communities apply annual rate increases. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to plan for current and future costs.

faqs.q: Does the average include everything?
faqs.a: Not always. Quoted averages usually reflect base care; private rooms, higher care levels, and extras like incontinence supplies can add to the bill. Always request a full fee schedule. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare true total costs.

page.quickAnswer: Alzheimer's care facilities are secured memory care communities staffed for Alzheimer's disease, with 24-hour supervision and dementia-trained teams. The 2026 national median cost is about $6,690 per month (A Place for Mom). Medicare doesn't cover room and board. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to find options near you.
page.heroSubhead: Locate trusted Alzheimer's care communities near home and understand exactly what they offer.
page.intro: Searching for Alzheimer's care facilities near you? This page explains what to look for and how 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you find the right fit for free.

sections.h2: What do Alzheimer's care facilities provide?
sections.body: Alzheimer's care facilities — a form of memory care — provide secured, wander-safe residences, 24-hour supervision, and staff trained specifically in Alzheimer's behaviors. Residents get help with bathing, dressing, grooming, and medications, plus meals, housekeeping, and structured activities designed to ease anxiety and maintain function. Many communities use specialized layouts, calming color schemes, and therapies tailored to memory loss. The emphasis is on safety, routine, and dignity as the disease progresses. Because Alzheimer's care is specialized, it costs more than standard assisted living. For help finding quality facilities near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How do I choose an Alzheimer's care facility?
sections.body: Evaluate communities on safety (secured exits, staffing ratios day and night), specialization (genuine Alzheimer's training and programming), transparency (all fees in writing), and proximity for family visits. Tour at different times, watch how staff interact with residents, and review state inspection reports. Ask how the facility handles wandering, agitation, medical emergencies, and late-stage care so the family won't need to move again. Trust what you see and smell. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you shortlist and compare Alzheimer's care facilities near you and understand how to pay — all at no cost.

faqs.q: How do I find Alzheimer's care facilities near me?
faqs.a: Identify care needs, budget, and location, then tour secured communities and confirm Alzheimer's-trained staffing and clear fees. The easiest way is calling 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help locating and comparing facilities near you.

faqs.q: Are Alzheimer's care facilities the same as memory care?
faqs.a: Yes. Alzheimer's care is a type of memory care — secured communities with dementia-trained staff serving people with Alzheimer's and related dementias. The terms are often used interchangeably. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare options near you.

faqs.q: How much do Alzheimer's care facilities cost?
faqs.a: In 2026, Alzheimer's care costs $4,000 to $11,000 per month, with a national median near $6,690 (A Place for Mom). Price varies by location and care level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for local estimates.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover Alzheimer's care facilities?
faqs.a: No. Medicare doesn't pay for room and board in an Alzheimer's care facility, but it covers medically necessary services like doctor visits, cognitive assessments, and short-term skilled nursing after a hospital stay. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review coverage.

The 2026 national median cost is about $6,690 per month (A Place for Mom). Medicare doesn't cover room and board. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to find options near you.

page.intro: Looking for dementia care facilities near you? This page covers what to look for and how 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you find the right community for free.

sections.h2: What do dementia care facilities offer?
sections.body: Dementia care facilities — also called memory care — provide secured residences that prevent wandering, 24-hour supervision, and staff trained in dementia behaviors. Residents receive help with daily activities, medication management, meals, housekeeping, and structured programs that reduce confusion and agitation. Designs often include simple layouts, secured outdoor spaces, and sensory or reminiscence therapies. The focus is keeping residents safe, calm, and engaged as dementia advances, whether the cause is Alzheimer's, vascular dementia, Lewy body, or another type. Because this care is specialized, it costs more than assisted living. To find quality facilities near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How do I compare dementia care facilities near me?
sections.body: Compare on safety (secured exits, day and night staffing ratios), specialization (true dementia training and programming), cost transparency (every fee in writing), and location for family visits. Tour at different times, observe resident wellbeing, and check state inspection records. Ask how the facility manages wandering, agitation, falls, and progression to late-stage care so your loved one can age in place. A slightly farther community may be worth it for stronger care. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you weigh these factors and understand how to pay, all at no cost.

faqs.q: How do I find dementia care facilities near me?
faqs.a: Define care needs, budget, and location, then tour secured communities and confirm dementia-trained staffing and clear pricing. The simplest path is calling 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help finding and comparing facilities near you.

faqs.q: Are dementia care and memory care the same?
faqs.a: Yes. Dementia care is another name for memory care — secured communities with trained staff serving people with dementia, including Alzheimer's. The terms are used interchangeably. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare options near you.

faqs.q: How much do dementia care facilities cost?
faqs.a: In 2026, dementia care costs $4,000 to $11,000 per month, with a national median near $6,690 (A Place for Mom). Cost varies by location, room type, and care level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for local estimates.

faqs.q: Does insurance cover dementia care facilities?
faqs.a: Medicare doesn't cover custodial dementia care, but Medicaid HCBS waivers, long-term care insurance, and VA Aid and Attendance may help. Coverage depends on your state and situation. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review what applies to you.

page.h1: Memory Care vs Assisted Living Cost
page.quickAnswer: Memory care costs 15–25% more than assisted living. In 2026, assisted living runs a median near $5,419 per month while memory care is about $6,690 (A Place for Mom). The gap pays for secured environments, dementia-trained staff, and higher supervision. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to compare.
page.heroSubhead: See exactly how memory care and assisted living costs differ in 2026 — and which fits your needs.
page.intro: Comparing memory care vs assisted living cost helps families budget correctly. This page breaks down the 2026 difference and how 1-800-MEDIGAP helps.

sections.h2: How do memory care and assisted living costs compare?
sections.body: Memory care consistently costs more than assisted living. In 2026, the median for assisted living is about $5,419 per month, while memory care runs roughly $6,690 — a difference of 15–25%. The extra cost covers secured, wander-safe buildings, staff trained in dementia, higher supervision ratios, and specialized therapies. Assisted living suits seniors who are largely independent but need some help; memory care is built for people with Alzheimer's or dementia who need a safe, structured environment. Some communities offer both, letting residents transition as needs change. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to compare real prices near you.

sections.h2: Which type of care does your loved one need?
sections.body: Choose based on safety and cognition, not just price. If your loved one is mostly independent and simply needs help with tasks like cooking, cleaning, or medication reminders, assisted living may be enough — and cheaper. If they wander, get confused or lost, forget to eat, or can't be safely left alone, memory care's secured environment is the safer choice despite the higher cost. Paying for assisted living when memory care is needed can put your loved one at risk. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you assess the right level of care and find communities that fit both the need and the budget.

faqs.q: How much more does memory care cost than assisted living?
faqs.a: Memory care costs 15–25% more than assisted living. In 2026, assisted living medians near $5,419 per month versus about $6,690 for memory care (A Place for Mom). The difference funds secured care and dementia-trained staff. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: Why is memory care more expensive than assisted living?
faqs.a: Memory care adds secured, wander-safe environments, dementia-trained staff, higher supervision ratios, and specialized therapies — all more labor-intensive than standard assisted living. That drives the 15–25% premium. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare both options.

faqs.q: Can I save money with assisted living instead of memory care?
faqs.a: Only if your loved one's needs match assisted living. Choosing it when memory care is needed risks their safety. If they wander or can't be left alone, memory care is the right choice despite cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to assess.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover either assisted living or memory care?
faqs.a: No. Medicare doesn't pay room and board for assisted living or memory care, as both are custodial care. It does cover related medical services. Medicaid, VA benefits, and insurance may help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to explore options.

faqs.q: Do some communities offer both assisted living and memory care?
faqs.a: Yes. Many communities offer both, often with a secured memory care wing, so residents can transition as needs change without leaving familiar surroundings. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find communities offering both near you.

page.metaDescription: Memory care is secured residential care for Alzheimer's and dementia. Learn what it includes and costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.

It costs more than assisted living, with a 2026 national median near $6,690 per month (A Place for Mom). Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to learn more.
page.heroSubhead: Understand what memory care is, what it includes, and whether it's right for your loved one.
page.intro: What is memory care? This page explains how it works, what it includes, and how 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you find the right community.

sections.h2: What is memory care and who is it for?
sections.body: Memory care is a specialized form of long-term residential care for people living with Alzheimer's disease, dementia, or other cognitive impairment. It provides secured (often locked or alarmed) environments to prevent wandering, 24-hour supervision, staff trained in dementia behaviors, and structured daily routines that reduce confusion and agitation. Residents receive help with bathing, dressing, grooming, medications, and meals, plus therapies designed to maintain function and quality of life. Memory care can be a standalone community or a secured wing within assisted living. It's for seniors whose cognitive decline makes living safely at home or in standard assisted living no longer possible. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to learn more.

sections.h2: How is memory care different from other senior care?
sections.body: Memory care differs from assisted living and nursing homes in focus. Assisted living serves mostly independent seniors who need light help; it lacks the security and dementia training memory care provides. Nursing homes deliver round-the-clock skilled medical care for complex health conditions. Memory care sits in between — more secure and specialized than assisted living, but not as medically intensive as a nursing home. Its defining features are wander-safe design, dementia-trained staff, and structured programming. Because of this specialization, memory care costs 15–25% more than assisted living. 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you understand which level of care fits and find the right community.

faqs.q: What is memory care?
faqs.a: Memory care is secured residential care for people with Alzheimer's or dementia, offering 24-hour supervision, dementia-trained staff, wander-safe design, and structured routines. It costs more than assisted living, with a 2026 national median near $6,690 per month (A Place for Mom). Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: What's the difference between memory care and assisted living?
faqs.a: Memory care adds secured environments, dementia-trained staff, and higher supervision for people with cognitive decline, while assisted living serves more independent seniors. Memory care costs 15–25% more. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare both.

faqs.q: What services does memory care include?
faqs.a: Memory care includes a secured room, meals, 24-hour supervision, help with daily activities and medications, housekeeping, laundry, and structured cognitive activities. Higher care levels may add fees. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to learn what's included near you.

faqs.q: Who needs memory care?
faqs.a: Seniors with Alzheimer's, dementia, or other cognitive impairment who can no longer live safely at home or in standard assisted living — especially those who wander, get confused, or can't be left alone — benefit from memory care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an assessment.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for memory care?
faqs.a: No. Medicare doesn't cover memory care room and board, classifying it as custodial care. It does cover medically necessary services like doctor visits and short-term skilled nursing after a hospital stay. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to review coverage.
